free_text: Measurement of double differential neutron emission
cross sections of Fe induced by 14.1 MeV neutrons
Pulsed 140 keV deuteron beam on tritium target
in a 1.5 mm thick and 30 cm long stainless steel
The neutron pulse width was about 1.8 ns at 1 MHz
repetition rate.
tubing.
The incident neutron energy was derived by a Monte
Carlo calculation.
Cylindrical natural iron (3 cm diam., 7 cm long)
at 17 cm from the neutron production target and
90 deg relative to the deuteron beam axis.
sample rotation technique provides the capability of
measuring scattering neutrons over an angular range
from 20 deg to 160 deg.
The flight path was 10 m.
To reduce the gamma-ray background.
To select threshold proerply.
BC-501A liquid scintillator (10 cm thick,
25.0 cm diam.)
Polyethylene sample of the same dimension as that of
the iron sample at 25 deg.
The neutron flux was also monitored by a three
scintillator detector system positioned above the
reaction plane.
The background was determined by measuring the 'sam-
p1e-out' spectrum after each 'sample-in' measurement.
Corrections have been made to the number of neutrons
incident on the iron sample relative to the polyethy-
lene sample at each scattering angle, for air scatte-
ring in flight, for multiple scattering in the iron
and polyethylene samples, for the continuous low ener-
gy part of the incident neutron spectrum.
Obtained from authors via private communication

statistical error.
The systematic error for each DDX value was
obtained from the following estimated uncertainties
summed in quadrature:
neutron detector efficiency              (3%)
variation of incident neutron flux       (2%)
hydrogen monitor cross section           (1%)
estimation of hydrogen scattering yield  (2%)
multiple scattering correction of
polyethylene and iron samples                (6-13%)
air and aluminium interaction effect     (1%)
On. 002-014 merged into 002.
Directly integrating DDX data
The total error obtained from the total
errors of the separate DDX data.
